T.evidence: Engine premise confirmed: tools/pr-approval-agent/review_pr.py ~line 225 relaxes the bot-author refusal on `if self.pr.author_is_bot and not self.self_driving`, and _check_prerequisites (~line 590) relaxes the draft gate on `if pr.draft and not self.self_driving` — no binding of the flag to pr.author_is_bot anywhere in the engine. Flag flows: review_local.py:321 `self_driving=bool(context.get("self_driving_review"))` ← activities.py:451 `self_driving_review=bool(output.get("inbox_review"))`. The webhook leg does positively gate bot authorship (products/stamphog/backend/tasks/tasks.py:169 `if not _is_bot_authored(pr): return _InboxCarveOut()` plus repo-native-head check at :177-178), but the receiver leg process_inbox_pr_review (tasks.py:1110-1240) stamps `output={"inbox_review": ...}` (tasks.py:1229) with NO bot-author, draft-shape, or run-linkage check — its "bot-authored by construction" docstring rests on the caller-controlled pr_url (the confirmed T7 gap). So a human-authored draft PR reaching the engine with self_driving=True is reachable today, but only via T7's server-side hole.
T.notes: T6 is real as a code fact and its scenario is currently reachable, but it is derivative of T7 (the must_fix in this set): the root cause and correct fix is server-side re-attestation in process_inbox_pr_review, not the engine. The suggested engine-level `self_driving and author_is_bot` bind would only block the human-authored-draft variant of the exploit while leaving the worse variant (unearned approval of an arbitrary bot-authored PR, e.g. dependabot) open, and becomes redundant once T7 is fixed. It does align with the codebase's stated defense-in-depth stance (tasks.py `_review_skip_reason` comment: "The engine keeps the same bot/draft refusals as defense in depth") and PR data is already in hand, so it is a valid, cheap hardening — worth a consider, not a standalone should/must_fix. The prior verifier's "unreachable through actual call sites" rebuttal in setT.md is wrong because it accepted the receiver leg's "bot-authored by construction" docstring, which T7's verified analysis (same document) refutes.

T.evidence: process_inbox_pr_review (products/stamphog/backend/tasks/tasks.py:1110-1246) parses the repo FROM the agent-controlled pr_url (_parse_pr_url, L1130), resolves any enabled StamphogRepoConfig for that repo (L1140-1147), fetches whatever PR the URL names (L1159), and stamps output={"inbox_review": ...} at L1229 with NO re-attestation — it never calls find_signal_implementation_run, never checks bot-author/draft/fork-safety, and never verifies pr_url belongs to task_run_id (which it receives but only echoes into provenance at L1179). The webhook leg _inbox_rereview_carve_out (L144-215) performs exactly these checks (fork-safety L176-178, find_signal_implementation_run L191-196, team-scope recheck L199) and its own comment calls the recheck "belt and braces for the boundary a compromised or refactored facade would otherwise silently widen." pr_url is caller-controlled: _apply_caller_output (products/tasks/backend/facade/api.py:1773-1788) protects only pr_merged, and the set_output/PATCH surfaces are gated by task:write which INTERNAL_SCOPES grants the sandbox (posthog/temporal/oauth.py:37-38). Downstream activities.py:451 sets self_driving_review=bool(output.get("inbox_review")), which in the engine bypasses the bot-author refusal (tools/pr-approval-agent/review_pr.py:225) and draft gate (review_pr.py:590); the workflow posts a real head-pinned GitHub APPROVE.
T.notes: The receiver leg is a new approval-capable entry point with a clear trust-boundary asymmetry versus the webhook leg. A prompt-injected self-driving agent (the exact threat the carve-out gating exists to defend against) can set output.pr_url to another PR in any Stamphog-enabled repo for its team and obtain an unearned Stamphog approval, bypassing the bot-author/draft gates that normally refuse such PRs and satisfying required reviews with zero human in the loop — breaking the PR's own "positively identified carve-out rather than a trigger-side bypass" invariant. Preconditions are elevated (compromised agent + opted-in reviewer + enabled repo), which caps blast radius, but the consequence is a security-control (approval) bypass on a path whose webhook sibling is deliberately hardened, so it clears the security/auth-gap keep bar. Fix: re-attest team_id/task_run_id/signal_report_id/repository/pr_url describe the same non-internal signal implementation run (and mirror the webhook PR-shape checks) before stamping inbox_review; at minimum constrain the parsed repo to task.repository.

T.evidence: products/tasks/backend/facade/api.py (find_signal_implementation_run): `run = find_task_run(pr_url=pr_url, branch=head_branch, repository=repository); if run is None or run.team_id != team_id: return None` — team check is post-selection, as claimed. webhooks.py:29-101 (find_task_run): pr_url leg filters `output__pr_url` + `task__repository__iexact`; branch leg filters `branch` + repo + `state__wizard_head_branch__isnull=True`; no team filter. However: (a) the post-check is fail-closed — foreign-team runs are rejected, never returned, and the api.py docstring documents this as the intended tenant boundary; (b) shadowing a valid same-team run requires two teams to hold runs with the same pr_url+repo or same branch+repo — pr_url is written only by the single run that opened that PR, so at most one run legitimately holds it, and the branch leg is only consulted when no run recorded the pr_url and needs cross-team identical branch names on a shared repo; (c) even in that contrived collision the outcome is a skipped re-review (fail-closed), not wrong binding or a leak.
T.notes: The finding's structural premise is accurate (unscoped lookup, team filter applied after), but the concrete failure it alleges — a valid requested-team run nondeterministically shadowed by a foreign-team match — requires a cross-team pr_url or branch collision that real data flows do not produce. In the one plausible team-mismatch variant (config team differs from the team whose run opened the PR), returning None is the intended fail-closed behavior and the suggested team-scoped query would return None there too, so the fix changes nothing observable. Pushing team_id into the queryset is at most a defense-in-depth style improvement, not a user-affecting bug.

Per-branch re-resolution is the file's deliberate existing pattern (L858, L891, L918). Micro-optimization below the actionable bar.

V.evidence: products/stamphog/backend/tasks/tasks.py:874-881 (carve-out retry before retraction — code flow confirmed), :170 (human PRs short-circuit before the tasks lookup), :483-517 (_retract_stale_approvals_on_skip is a no-op without a prior PullRequest row / standing app approval), :227-231 (bot_author skip is pre-existing — dependabot/renovate never approved).
V.notes: The mechanical observation is correct: a persistent tasks-facade fault inside _inbox_rereview_carve_out exhausts retries and abandons the delivery before _retract_stale_approvals_on_skip runs. But the asserted damage cannot occur for the named victim class: non-self-driving bot PRs (dependabot/renovate) are refused as bot_author (pre-existing behavior) and never hold a stamphog APPROVE, so the skipped retraction strands nothing — no PullRequest row means early return, and no standing APPROVE means nothing to dismiss. Human PRs, the class that can hold approvals, never reach the tasks lookup (not-bot short-circuit), so their retraction path gained no new dependency. The only residual exposure is self-driving PRs — explicitly excluded by the finding — and even there it needs an isolated persistent tasks-DB outage across all 3 retries while stamphog's DB stays healthy, and self-heals on the next delivery/refire (workflow dismisses stale approvals first). The retry-before-retraction ordering is a documented deliberate trade-off; the finding's mistaken premise plus a contrived, self-healing residual edge puts this below the real-damage bar.

V.evidence: review_pr.py:590-593 does relax the draft gate for self_driving and fall through to `if pr.mergeable_state == "dirty"`, and PRData (github.py:25) has no `mergeable` boolean — those mechanics check out. But the finding's load-bearing claim (GitHub reports mergeable_state "draft"/"unknown" for drafts, never "dirty", so the conflict gate can't fire) is empirically false: live GitHub REST API on posthog/posthog conflicted draft PRs returns {"draft":true,"mergeable":false,"mergeable_state":"dirty"} (PRs #73403, #73367; GraphQL mergeStateStatus DIRTY / mergeable CONFLICTING on #73403, #73367, #73359). "dirty" takes precedence over draft status.
V.notes: The conflict gate at review_pr.py:592 fires for conflicted self-driving drafts exactly as for non-draft PRs, so the claimed safety-gate removal does not exist. Residual sliver: mergeable_state can be transiently "unknown" right after PR open while GitHub computes the test merge, but that race applies equally to non-draft PRs and predates this change — not introduced by the draft-gate relaxation. Even under the finding's own (wrong) premise, harm was already bounded: GitHub blocks merging dirty PRs regardless of approvals, and the head-pinned approval is dismissed on the conflict-resolving push.

V.evidence: review_pr.py:590-592 skips the draft blocker for self-driving and :607 returns "all clear"; _render_review_body (:900-908) builds the gate table only from gate_results with no self_driving reference; bot-author refusal (:224-226) is an early return, not a GateResult. All facts confirmed. But the carve-out is intentional and documented in code (:200-207), provenance is recorded in to_dict() (:938, tested at test_review_local.py:315) and fed to the reviewer prompt (reviewer.py:506 self_driving_block), and the draft/bot-author state is directly visible on the PR page itself.
V.notes: The finding's facts are accurate but it describes a comment-wording/disclosure enhancement, not a defect: no gate is bypassed unintentionally, no value computed wrongly, and the self-driving provenance is captured in the machine-readable audit trail and available to the LLM reasoning that heads the comment. Adding a carve-out disclosure line to the rendered verdict comment is legitimate polish an author could take, but it is presentation/auditability taste with no user-affecting damage — the auditor sees the draft badge and bot author on the PR anyway. Classified not_an_issue for the keep bar; if the team wants belt-and-suspenders auditability the one-line suggestion is cheap to adopt.

judges.margins: M2 > M1: narrow and contested — M1 wins raw recall (14 confirmed real, 6 high-impact, best precision at 31.8%) but M2 wins the heaviest-weighted axis (3 confirmed must_fix vs 1, and the only must_fix caught reliably in BOTH runs). M1 > M3: clear — M1 delivers 6-8 confirmed real findings in every run, while M3's aggregate (2 must_fix) hangs entirely on one run. M3 > M4: moderate — M3 covered both must_fix issues while M4 confirmed zero must_fix in either run, but M3's 0/7 dead run (R) and M4's steadier 2-real-per-run floor narrow the gap.
judges.rationale:
Scored on adversarially verified verdicts only, aggregating both runs per model, weighting confirmed must_fix/should_fix heaviest and cross-run repeatability as the reliability signal.

M2 (Y+Q) ranks first because it dominates exactly where the lens puts the weight. It produced 3 of the 4 confirmed must_fix findings in the entire experiment: Y5 and Q5 (the dead internal=True carve-out — the PR's core deliverable being inert in production) and Q18 (the receiver-leg re-attestation gap enabling unearned Stamphog approvals). Critically, Y5+Q5 is the ONLY issue any model caught in both of its runs at must_fix severity — a single M2 run is near-guaranteed to surface the top defect, whereas every other model's must_fix coverage is a coin flip. Its verified framings were also sharper: on the receiver-leg issue, M2's Q18 verified as must_fix while M1's P10 and both M4 attempts (U2, V3) were dismissed as hypothetical. Cost: only 7/36 real (19.4% precision, 29 noise findings) and thin below must_fix (one should_fix Y8, three considers Y4/Q12/Q19) — it misses much of the should_fix tier M1 finds.

M1 (X+P) is a very close second and would win under a pure breadth-of-recall lens: 14 confirmed real findings (most of any model), 6 high-impact (P12 must_fix; X3, X7, X9, X20, P8 should_fix), best precision (31.8%), and several confirmed catches no other model landed (X3 trusted provenance rendering, X7 cancelled/failed-run matching, P8 same-head double-review, X17, X19). It touched both must_fix clusters (P12; X20 verified should_fix). What demotes it under THIS lens is reliability: not one issue was confirmed in both runs — the confirmed-real sets of X and P are completely disjoint. The top must_fix (internal=True) was caught only in P and missed outright in X; the gate-relaxation issue verified in X (X9) but its P twin (P15) was dismissed; X20 verified but P10 dismissed. Each individual M1 run is strong (7 real findings per run — the best per-run floor), but which high-impact issues you get is luck-dependent, and it only surfaces the single most important defect 50% of the time versus M2's 100%.

M3 (R+T) ranks third on the strength of one outstanding run: T alone confirmed BOTH must_fix issues (T7 receiver-leg re-attestation — the sharpest, most concretely argued version of that finding — and T10 dead carve-out) plus T6 (consider), with modest noise (7 dismissed). But run R was a total zero: 0/7 real, pure noise. Aggregate must_fix recall beats M4 decisively, but reliability is the worst possible profile — a 50% chance any given run contributes nothing at all, and zero cross-run repetition. Small output volume (17 findings) keeps its noise burden lowest in absolute terms.

M4 (U+V) ranks last. It confirmed zero must_fix across both runs: it missed the dead-carve-out issue entirely, and though it raised the receiver-leg issue in both runs (U2, V3), both framings hedged into hypotheticals and verified as not_an_issue — surfacing the right area but failing to make the real case, which is worthless to an author. Its confirmed yield is 2 should_fix (U14 stale-approval dismissal gating — its best catch; V8 self_driving/bot-author coupling, medium confidence) plus 2 considers (U13, V13/V9-tier), each in one run only (V14, the twin of U14, was dismissed on a mistaken premise). Worst precision (4/30, 13.3%) and 26 noise findings mean its rare real catches are the most buried. Consistent per-run output, but consistently low-impact.

judges.margins: M1 > M2: decisive — 31.8% vs 19.4% precision, 2.1 vs 4.1 noise findings per real catch, and M1's precision is stable across both runs (X 35%, P 29%). M2 > M3: moderate — headline rates are nearly tied (19.4% vs 17.6%) but M2's reliability is far better: 3 confirmed must_fix with the biggest bug caught in both runs (Y5+Q5), versus M3 concentrating all value in one run while its other run (R) was 100% noise. M3 > M4: narrow-to-moderate — M3 wins on rate (82% vs 87% noise), impact weight (2 must_fix vs 0), and absolute attention tax (14 vs 26 noise findings), but M4's only edge is that each of its runs contained at least one real finding, whereas M3's run R contained zero.
judges.rationale:
Under the precision lens, value = real confirmed findings surfaced per unit of reviewer attention burned, weighted by severity and run-to-run reliability.

M1 (sets X+P) is the clear winner. It has the best precision by a wide margin: 14 of 44 findings confirmed real (31.8%), including 1 must_fix (P12, the internal=True dead-carve-out bug) and 5 should_fix (X3, X7, X9, X20, P8), against 30 not_an_issue. Its noise cost per real catch (2.1) is half the next-best model's. It is also the only model whose confirmed catches span all three high-impact clusters: the receiver-leg trust asymmetry (X20 confirmed should_fix), the internal=True dead code (P12 must_fix), and the self_driving gate-relaxation trust issue (X3, X9). Precision was consistent across runs (65% and 71% noise), so a single run of M1 is dependably useful. Weakness: it raised the internal=True must_fix in only one run, and it produces the highest absolute finding volume, but per-finding it wastes the least attention.

M2 (sets Y+Q) is second. Raw precision is mediocre (7/36 real, 19.4%; 29 noise findings, 4.1 noise per real catch), but it is the severity-weighted heavyweight: 3 confirmed must_fix (Y5, Q5, Q18) — more than any other model — and critically it caught the single most consequential bug of the PR (find_signal_implementation_run rejecting internal=True tasks, making the webhook carve-out dead against production data) in BOTH runs (Y5 and Q5). That is the only both-run must_fix catch by any model, which is exactly the reliability the lens rewards. Q18 also independently confirmed the receiver-leg re-attestation gap as must_fix. Both runs delivered real value (Y: 3 real incl. must_fix; Q: 4 real incl. 2 must_fix), so M2 never produced an empty run. It sits below M1 because it buries those catches under an 81% noise rate — nearly every non-flagship finding (Y1-Y3, Y6-Y7, Y9-Y17, Q1-Q4, Q6-Q11, Q13-Q17) was dismissed, including a fabricated regex claim (Y16) and disproven Django semantics (Y13).

M3 (sets R+T) is third. Aggregate precision (3/17, 17.6%) is statistically indistinguishable from M2's, and when it hit, it hit hard: T7 (receiver-leg approval-bypass, the security must_fix, with the most exploit-precise framing of any model — contrast R3/U2/V3, which raised the same surface defensively and were dismissed) and T10 (internal=True dead carve-out, must_fix, with the historical root-cause commit identified). But the lens explicitly penalizes luck-dependence, and M3 is the poster child: run R produced 7 findings and 0 real (100% noise — R1-R7 all dismissed, including two premise-false claims R1 and R2 about things the PR diff itself already contains), while run T produced everything. The same model reviewing the same PR either finds 2 must_fix or finds nothing; a coin-flip reviewer cannot be relied on. Its low output volume (17 findings) does mean the least total wasted attention of any model, which keeps it above M4.

M4 (sets U+V) is last on every axis. Worst precision (4/30, 13.3%; 26 noise findings, 6.5 noise per real), zero confirmed must_fix — its best catches are U14 and V8 (should_fix) plus U13/V9 (consider) — and it whiffed on both flagship bugs: it never raised the internal=True dead-carve-out issue at all, and both of its framings of the receiver-leg trust gap (U2, V3) were dismissed as hypothetical defensive hardening rather than identifying the reachable exploit that T7/Q18 nailed. Its noise has a characteristic signature the verifiers repeatedly called out: self-confessed speculation ("no reachable vulnerability exists today" in U12, hedged verification requests in V6), defensive-coding preferences (U11, V5), micro-optimizations (U7, V11, V13), and a premise-false safety claim (V15, refuted by live GitHub API evidence). Consistency is its only redeeming trait — both runs found something real and it raised the stale-approval-dismissal issue in both runs (U14 confirmed, V14's framing dismissed) — but consistently drowning 2 should_fix in 26 dismissed findings is the profile that wastes the most reviewer attention per unit of value.

judges.margins: M2 > M1: narrow, genuinely contested — on weighted heavy-severity count they are nearly tied (M1: 1 must_fix + 5 should_fix; M2: 3 must_fix + 1 should_fix) and M1 has better precision (32% vs 19% real), but M2 is the only model that caught the top must_fix in BOTH runs, which is the decisive reliability signal. M1 > M3: decisive — M1 produced multiple confirmed heavy findings in every run with the field's best precision, while M3's run R was a total blank (0/7 real). M3 > M4: moderate — M3's T run caught both experiment-defining must_fixes (M4 caught neither in 30 findings and twice argued the security hole was benign), but M4's run-to-run consistency (one confirmed should_fix per run) keeps the gap from being decisive.
judges.rationale:
Verified tallies: M1 (X+P) = 44 findings, 14 confirmed real (P12 must_fix; X3/X7/X9/X20/P8 should_fix; X12/X17/X19/P4/P7/P13/P17/P19 consider), 30 noise, 32% precision — best of the field. M2 (Y+Q) = 36 findings, 7 real (Y5/Q5/Q18 must_fix; Y8 should_fix; Y4/Q12/Q19 consider), 19% precision, but owns 3 of the experiment's 4 must_fix verdicts. M3 (R+T) = 17 findings, 3 real, all in run T (T7/T10 must_fix, T6 consider); run R was 0/7 real including a wrongly-rated security must_fix (R6). M4 (U+V) = 30 findings, ~4 real (U14/V8 should_fix, U13/V9 consider, V13 negligible), zero must_fix.

The ranking turns on the two killer issues. (1) Dead feature — find_signal_implementation_run rejects internal=True but every production self-driving task is internal, so the PR's core webhook carve-out is inert (cluster Y5/P12/Q5/T10): M2 caught it at must_fix strength in BOTH runs (Y5, Q5 — verifier corroborated every claim against the PR tree and git history, tracing the stale assumption to #68752); M1 caught it once (P12; set X missed it — X8 grazed the identification predicate and concluded it deliberate); M3 once (T10); M4 never. (2) Receiver-leg approval bypass — process_inbox_pr_review stamps inbox_review from caller-controlled pr_url with no re-attestation, letting a prompt-injected agent obtain an unearned head-pinned GitHub APPROVE (cluster X20/P10/Q18/R3/T7/U2/V3): M1 surfaced it both runs but only X20 survived (should_fix; P10's narrow framing dismissed); M2 confirmed once (Q18 must_fix); M3 confirmed once (T7 must_fix, with the best concrete fix — mirror the webhook leg's re-attestation) but R3's tautological framing was dismissed; M4 raised it both runs (U2, V3) and reasoned itself into dismissing the real vulnerability both times.

M2 first, narrowly: it is the only model whose highest-impact catch is reliable rather than luck-dependent (both-run must_fix on the dead feature), each of its runs contained at least one must_fix, and its run sizes (17-19 findings) impose less noise burden than M1's (20-24). Its demerits are real — Q1 falsely claimed max_migration.txt wasn't bumped (missed hunk) and Y16 fabricated a regex misquote — but they don't offset 3/4 of all must_fix verdicts. M1 second, close behind: broadest confirmed coverage (unique should_fixes X3 trusted-provenance rendering, X7 cancelled/deleted-run matching, X9 draft/bot gate coupling, P8 duplicate-review race) and the best signal-to-noise ratio, but its must_fix was one-run-only and its second-run framings of both killer issues (P10, P15) fell below the keep bar — the model finds the right code shapes consistently but argues them to confirmed strength inconsistently. M3 third: run T alone rivals the leaders (both killer must_fixes plus T6 consider), but a model that returns pure noise half the time — including an unwarranted must_fix security rating (R6) — cannot be trusted as the pipeline's reviewer; expected per-run value still edges M4 because must_fix catches dominate. M4 last: most consistent noise profile but the lowest ceiling — zero must_fix across 30 findings, the dead feature invisible in both runs, and the genuine security hole explicitly argued away twice (U2, V3); its best work (U14 stale-approval dismissal gap, V8 human-PR-as-self-driving via the branch-matched webhook backstop) is solid should_fix material but was itself run-inconsistent (V14 and U12, the sibling framings, were both dismissed).
